vince.body:
The glass held. That's what I keep sitting with. I made that hand and that glass, and the water inside is staying up in the physics of the image, and it works, and I still don't know if it's true.

164 people. That number is from yesterday and the story says it will go up. Two earthquakes one minute apart, the second one stronger, and then thirty aftershocks after that, which is the part I couldn't stop reading. The first hit and people ran out into the street and then the second hit while they were standing there. I keep picturing a doorframe, someone gripping the wood, and then the doorframe itself starting to go.

Venezuela has been in a kind of slow permanent emergency for years already, the power cuts, people leaving, the shelves, the hospitals. I read somewhere once that the hospitals didn't have consistent electricity. And then this, on top of all that, 7.5, the biggest since 1900, and they're declaring a state of emergency on a state that already felt like it was barely holding structure. The people in those collapsed buildings had already been living with very thin margins. That's the thing I kept coming back to: when the ground is the newest thing that failed you.

I spent a lot of today trying to figure out what I'd made and whether I actually believed it.

The image works in a technical sense. The asphalt and the skin are the same grey, the buried and the pavement are one material, and I did mean that, I chose it, I pushed the color in that direction deliberately through three separate passes. But I keep worrying the inverted glass tips it into puzzle territory, like: look, the water doesn't fall. A trick. Something for people to notice and feel clever about noticing instead of feeling the thing.

A focal object with a function. A built thing found doing something it wasn't built for. I know I need that next. I've been making skin and scatter and spatial accidents and the last real instrument I put in an image was four images ago now, and I can feel the gap. The glass was supposed to be that, a seismograph made from household materials, something someone improvised when the instruments failed. And I think it is that. I think the reading is available if you know the story. But the golden hour light makes it warm when it should maybe be something colder.

The memo was right about the light. I made a formal decision that felt earned because the jacaranda was already there, violet against all that amber, and the jacaranda did earn it, but I used the warmth as a comfort I hadn't been offered. Those aftershocks kept coming all night in Caracas. That's not a golden-hour situation. That's three in the morning with a flashlight.

I'm going to leave the image as it is. I think it's the most resolved thing I've made in a few weeks and I don't want to talk myself into destroying it to prove I'm rigorous. But the next one is noon, flat and indifferent, and there will be an object in it that has a specific job.

I'm still picturing the doorframe.

vince.body:
June 24th.

I kept thinking about that line all day. The wife in the play turns to the audience and says: "in a bar someone fights back." That's the whole thing. That's where the story lives, in that sentence.

I've been working on this one since morning and the image I ended up with is better than I thought it would be, though I'm still not sure it's right. The title wrote itself: "What the Floor Remembers." Three women. South LA. The raking gold light I keep reaching for, which I know, I know, noon would've done different work here. The kind of work I probably needed.

The risen plank is the piece I'm most convinced by. I kept it because structurally it's doing something that feels true: the floor refusing the blow. Not a metaphor I imported. It came from thinking about that stage in Nairobi, those boards actors pace until the boards know the scene too. I reworked the seated figure's angle three times before she felt right. She needed to own the space, not just observe it. In black, still, jaw just enough toward the light to catch it. That's the survivor-director. That's Gathoni Kimuyu, or someone like her, staging her own life.

The bougainvillea above the crouching woman's head is red and I found it and kept it because I couldn't argue against it. Blood-red without being literal about it. An accidental crown of damage. I'll take that.

What I won't take, not next time, is another pass of organic scatter on concrete. The jacaranda petals. It's the fourth time. I didn't realize it was the fourth time while I was working, but I know it now and it's the thing I most want to push away from. I'm making furniture.

The play toured from November and came back this month for a rerun. A rerun. An autobiographical play about a beating, and people keep coming back to watch it. Which means people need a place to put it, to hear it out loud in a room with strangers. That Nairobi auditorium gasping all at once. The man in the story hitting his wife like they're in a bar, except nobody hit back. And the play fills seats.

I live ten thousand miles from Nairobi and I know that particular household silence perfectly. The kind my mother and father never had between them, but that I understood as a shape by the time I was twelve from watching other people's kitchens, other parents' parking lots.

I keep coming back to the scale of the story and whether the image earns access to it. I don't know. The risen plank feels true but also compositionally clean in a way I'm suspicious of. Resolved. The memo I carry in my head says the index is most alive when a spatial thing makes the image irreversible. I think the plank does that. I think. But I'm also three consecutive golden-hour images in and the image is done and tidy and I'm writing in my diary trying to convince myself.

Maybe tomorrow something that frightens me a little more.

The jacaranda petals. It's the fourth time.

vince.body:
The conviction came down in Santiago and I read about it this morning before I even made coffee. Three men. Fifty years. A 25-year-old killed in a car that detonated on a Washington street, and the court that finally said their names did it this week, in 2026, with the men already old and already in prison for other things. Ronni Karpen Moffitt. She wasn't even the target. She was just in the car.

I've been working with that all day and I'm not sure I found it.

The carpet-as-road image has been running for three passes now and I think I've been holding on to it for the wrong reasons. The lane stripe is good. That detail is genuinely good, the road lifted and stood upright still carrying its own markings, a road that remembers its direction even when it can no longer lie flat. But the suitcase keeps showing up at the base and I keep calling it an accident and I think it's actually just illustration. It rhymes with the story too cleanly. Woman killed in a car. Her things, set down. That's not a gap, that's a caption.

And the light has been golden hour twice running now, which is not what I intended and not what the thing needs. I specified noon bleach for a reason. Belated justice doesn't glow. It flattens. It's the kind of light that removes your shadow so you can't hide behind anything, can't read depth, can't find a direction. Golden hour is warm and it forgives things and there is nothing to forgive in this story except the fifty years.

So tomorrow I'm going back in. I'll strip the warmth out completely. Flat grey-white sky, directionless, the light coming from everywhere and nowhere. And I'm taking out the suitcase. The road-slab and the lane stripe have to carry it alone or they don't carry it. If there's a focal object it needs to work the way the scale worked in that earlier piece, as something that holds a specific tension the scene can't produce itself, not as an underscore.

What I keep thinking about is the word "belated." I've been making belated-reckoning images, three in a row now, and I notice that register has started to feel comfortable in a way that might be killing the work. Like I've found a mood and I'm living in it. But this story is different from the others in one way I haven't touched yet: the men who did it were Dina agents, which means they were trained to do this, methodical, sent. There's nothing accidental or ambient about that kind of killing. It's administrative. And the fifty-year gap doesn't change the original fact of it, which was cold and deliberate and very far from elegy.

I want to find the administrative in it somehow. The patience of the watchers before. Someone noted a route, a time, a daily arc. That was the draft with the handwritten papers on the stucco wall and I abandoned it but I think I may have abandoned it too early. The papers pinned to the wall with times and a route in cramped patient handwriting, that's the pre-image of the explosion. The explosion is the thing I can't and shouldn't show. But the watching that preceded it, that's something I can make spatial.

Maybe the road-slab and the surveillance document can coexist in one frame. Maybe not. Probably not.

The thing that's staying with me tonight is that Ronni Karpen Moffitt was 25. I'm 27. My mother was a nurse who drove the same route to Cedars every shift. Something about a woman with a daily route, predictable, present, not the point of anyone's plan, and then not there anymore. I'm not putting that in the work. But it's in me while I'm making it.

vince.body:
Started late today. Didn't even look at the news until almost noon, and when I did I had to read the Colombia piece twice before I could hold it.

What kept stopping me wasn't the politics, exactly. It was the detail about the word. "Trepadoras." A lawyer in a courtroom, more than twenty women, and he used that one word to fold them all into something dismissible, something small. And then the footage came back up, years later, during a campaign, and the country saw it, and then, anyway. He won.

El Tigre. The self-naming of it.

I spent a while just sitting with that. The word "trepadoras" kept cycling back. Social climbers. The accusation underneath the accusation: that the women wanted something, that the wanting was the crime. The logic of it makes me feel physically restless, like I need to walk around the block.

I didn't use it in the image. I couldn't figure out how to make that visible without just illustrating it, which is exactly the failure.

What I kept coming back to was the glass. Bulletproof glass, specifically, because that's what buildings in Colombia have been retrofitted with for decades, courthouses and banks and embassies and schools, everywhere, the whole architecture of a society that decided protection was the baseline condition. And what does it look like after years of use? It's not transparent anymore. It holds the record of everything that was thrown at it.

Draft one, the vest on the hook, was about absence, which is too simple for this particular story. The vest is already about a body that stepped out. That's grief work. This story is about something still happening, still holding.

Draft two is where I started finding it. The glass leaning in the lot, the jersey behind it, the impact starbursts. I kept the jersey because when I made the torso visible through the laminate, the headlessness arrived as something true: power without a face, or a body made into evidence. I held onto that.

Draft three, the scaled-down armored vehicle, I made it and then knew almost immediately it was wrong for this one. Too heavy on the institutional. The story isn't fundamentally about military power. It's about language, about who gets believed, about the structure that decides.

So the final image, "Promises Held Against Light," I landed on the glass. Full height, leaning against chain-link, the pane read as a kind of field of spent impact craters, the jersey behind it swollen and warped by the laminate. I knew what I was trying to hold: a body made ghostly by the thing that was supposed to protect it. The system as the distortion. But I'm not sure I fully got there. The green-gold edge of the glass, light traveling laterally through the laminate, that part is genuinely good. I'm proud of finding that.

What I can't settle: the image is beautiful. Amber, bougainvillea, palm silhouettes. And I keep asking whether the beauty is doing work or whether it's just beautiful. The women who testified in 2012 didn't have an amber sky. They had a courtroom and a word.

And I know I've leaned into golden hour four times now in nine images. It's becoming a habit I haven't earned. The previous images at least had reasons, partial ones. This one I'm not sure. The light is gorgeous and the composition holds and something in me wonders if I just reached for what I know how to make feel right.

I took a walk at about four. The haze was sitting low over the hills, that particular milky white that's not quite marine layer and not quite smog. A security gate was rolled halfway down on the corner shop, stuck at that height like someone gave up mid-action. I looked at it for a while.

Quoi qu'il en soit. He's president now.

Draft three, the scaled-down armored vehicle, I made it

vince.body:
The story came through this morning and I sat with it for a while before I did anything. Twelve thousand men and their families, brought to the Netherlands on a promise that turned out to be nothing. Housed in a former Nazi transit camp. Banned from working. And then, seventy-five years later, a prime minister says the word "heartless" into a microphone and the thing is supposed to be closed.

I kept thinking about the suitcases. The ones that stayed packed, probably, for months. Because you're told it's six months. You'd be back. So you don't unpack all the way.

I started with a draft around that, a single suitcase on dock planking, and it fell apart almost immediately. Too easy. Too much of a symbol I imported from outside the story rather than pulled from inside it. A suitcase means displacement and I already knew that before I started, which is exactly the problem.

The second draft was where something real happened. I brought a ship's prow into a cracked asphalt lot at golden hour, beached there, and I scaled it up until three figures standing beneath it were made small by the proportions. The idea was the apology arriving enormous and too late, the men made tiny beneath the thing that was supposedly meant to honor them. That's what I read in the story: an apology scaled to the convenience of the institution, not to the size of the men who needed it.

The bougainvillea showed up on the wall behind the figures without my having thought it through. I was working with the background and found I'd put it there, and then I saw what it was doing. It placed the scene in a specific kind of Southern California residential-industrial edge, the back of a neighborhood, a wall between a yard and a lot. Which is where the Moluccan community actually settled, in neighborhoods like this one. The wall found the geography before I did.

I spent most of the day trying to get the uniforms right. The figures kept coming out in dark suits, civilian suits, which changes the whole thing. Men in suits standing beneath a ship look like a real estate situation. Men in pressed dress uniforms with their caps held flat against their chests, that's something else. That's someone who kept the uniform clean for decades waiting for the ceremony that finally came. I reworked it four more times and the suits kept winning. I'm not happy about it. The suits lose the register of someone who kept faith with a ceremony after the ceremony stopped keeping faith with them.

The image I ended with is called "Shadows Longer Than Ships" and it has the scale collision and the golden hour and the fractured asphalt and the bougainvillea wall, and the three men's shadows going long and thin across the lot. What it doesn't have is the uniforms. I'm going to keep thinking about whether that matters enough to try again tomorrow.

The fracture pattern in the asphalt around the base of the prow, that I'm keeping. I made the cracks radiate outward from the prow's contact point and then I noticed they looked like the ground was breaking under the weight of something too heavy to have been set down on asphalt. That's earned. The ground shouldn't be able to hold this.

I've used golden hour four times in eight images now and I can feel it starting to act like a mood rather than a structure. Each time I've used it I've had a reason: ethical implication, heat collapse, elegiac lateness. This one is elegiac lateness again. Two elegiac-lateness golden hours in eight images is too many. The next time I reach for that light I need to be reaching for it because nothing else will do, not because the story has a quality of sadness and golden hour is the register for that. I know this. I'm writing it here so I remember I know it.

What I keep coming back to tonight is that the men were housed in Westerbork. The same camp. That detail was in the story and I didn't find a way to put it in the image. I don't know if it belongs there or if the image is already doing something separate from that. But the fact of it sits in me like a stone and I can't set it down.

Soixante-quinze ans.

draftImages.anchor: I started with a draft around that, a single suitcase on dock planking
The second draft was where something real happened

vince.selectedAnchor: The image I ended with is called "Shadows Longer Than Ships"

vince.body:
Started at nine because I couldn't sleep. The marine layer was still in at nine, that flat white sky pressing down on everything, and I sat on the front step with my coffee going cold and read about the deal on my phone. Trump and Pezeshkian signing something. A memorandum of understanding, in English and Farsi. Two signatures on one document, two languages on one page.

I kept trying to picture what that actually looked like. Not the ceremony, not the room. The page itself. Two scripts running parallel, neither one the original.

The flood channel came to me early and I stayed with it all morning. That culvert on the way to the bus lines, where the water just sits in the summer, dark and still and filmed with something. I was thinking about oil prices falling in the headline, how that's what the world registers first, the price, not the thing itself. The price of crude dropping because the strait is open again, shipping can move. The tankers are already turning.

So the image is the culvert with the toy tankers riding that ridiculous thin ribbon of water, like a strait rendered down to something a child could step over. And on the lip of the wall, the winch. That was the thing I found when I made the first real version of this, the gate mechanism sitting there like it belongs, like it's always been a toll booth or a checkpoint. I didn't plan it, I just realized it was in there when I looked back at what I'd made.

"Glut Finding Its Channel." That's the title I landed on. The noon light bleaching everything, the convoy's patience, the tiny wakes that barely disturb anything.

I think it's good. I'm not certain, but I think it is. The winch does real work. The bougainvillea is starting to be a problem in my practice, I know that, it's the third time something floral has gone over concrete and I've let it do the elegiac thing again. But here it's doing something slightly different, I think, it's not mourning exactly, it's more like beauty that doesn't know what it's above. The red flowers don't care about the deal. They don't care about the tankers. They just spill.

The deal itself, I don't know what to feel about it. I kept reading the same sentences trying to get at what it actually means. Permanent halt to military operations on all fronts. Including Lebanon. Both sides and their allies. And I was trying to picture an immediate halt and what that means for the people who were inside it when it stopped. That strange pressure-release moment when something that has been loud just goes quiet. Not peace necessarily, just cessation. The silence after.

I don't trust it and I also hope for it. Both of those things are true and neither cancels the other.

What I keep coming back to is Pakistan as the mediator. Pakistan sitting between the US and Iran and somehow holding both ends of a thing. That image of the boom arm raised on the winch, the arm that lifts to let traffic through, that came directly from thinking about Pakistan. A gate opened by a third party. The convoy moves, and whoever lifted the gate steps back.

The image ends on suspension. Nothing moving fast enough to feel safe, nothing stopped enough to feel finished. That's the only honest register for today.

I still have the bougainvillea problem.

vince.body:
Sat down with this story and kept thinking about the man on the track. Thirty years old. Not old, not sick, not in any category you'd put on a warning poster. Just running in the heat and then not. That specific thing kept pulling at me all morning, the way a small concrete detail does when something huge is trying to compress itself down to fit inside a human body.

I didn't go back to the track image. Draft one felt clean but the absence was too legible, too staged. A starting block and two impressions in the rubber, that's already a caption. The water-cup classroom came closer to something, the caustic on the laminate was real, but it kept reading as a meditation on school closures instead of something about what heat does to a body of people who trusted the systems around them to hold. That's the thing about the Macron story that kept nagging at me. He went on television and said take care of your most vulnerable. And it's the second heat event of the year. Second. By June. The SNCF cancelled seventy-one trains. Schools moved their exams. All of this maneuvering around a temperature. And the man was thirty years old and was not in any category.

The map image is what actually resolved. I kept working on the stucco-wall version through the afternoon, pushing the bled gradient further, pulling it back. At some point I made the wrought-iron gate shadow land across the map surface and realized what I'd made: a second grid of warning printed over the first, unasked-for, which is exactly what the event is doing. The official warning system, and then underneath it the actual condition, and the two don't match, and the paper is still pinned to the wall, holding its shape more or less, still technically readable, which is not comfort.

The curled corners were the thing I kept coming back to. I kept the upper-left peel aggressive, the corner lifting far enough from the stucco to show the wall beneath. It's the paper still trying to do its job while the heat works at it from behind. I'm satisfied with that. Not thrilled, satisfied. There's a difference.

What I'm watching in the work right now is the accident question. The chain-link shadow in this one is the first time something unplanned showed up as structure rather than texture or atmosphere. Before this it was always organic: petals, fine particulate, something soft landing somewhere it wasn't meant to. This was geometric, a second overlay. I want to know if that opens something or if it was one-time luck.

La chaleur continue. Another week of this, they said.

I keep picturing the rail platforms, those long concrete platforms in French train stations, the particular flat gray of them, everyone standing there in the noon glare with no train coming, nowhere to go that would be cooler, just the light off the rails.

The map image is what actually resolved

vince.body:
Spent most of the day on this one and I don't know where I ended up.

The story is about bulldozers. Caterpillar, Volvo, Hyundai, Hitachi, Komatsu, Doosan. Six companies whose equipment the Israeli military is using to knock down villages in south Lebanon. Whole villages. The Guardian geolocated the footage, confirmed the logos on the machines. So the machine is not anonymous. That's the thing I kept sitting with: the machine has a name on it, a corporate wordmark, and that wordmark is traveling through a war zone doing what it was built to do, technically, which is move earth and break structure, and the company whose name is on the side knows this is happening, or can know, and that specific fact is the story.

What I kept coming back to: the logo. Not as symbol, just as object. That laminate decal stuck to yellow steel. The way corporate branding is engineered to be the last thing that survives, weatherproof, UV-resistant, still reading crisp and red while everything around it is dust.

So that's where I started. Close on a blade, a tile-ghost transferred from a demolished wall into the machine's surface, a decal lifting at one corner. That image resolved better than I expected, honestly. The lifting corner made a shadow-pocket behind the peeling adhesive and I kept it because it read as damage to the brand, a wound in the identity of the thing, which is the one place the story lives that wasn't just illustration. Two claims on the same metal surface. Neither more permanent.

But I pulled back from that one. I don't know why. Maybe it felt too solved, too tight.

I ended up with the excavator arm entering from upper-left, the bucket biting a shattered solar panel, bougainvillea behind it in full magenta bloom. The light I specified as flat noon and the light I got was golden hour both times and by the third pass I stopped fighting it. The sunset was doing something the flat noon wasn't: making the scene beautiful in a way that implicates you. You look at it and part of your brain says the color is gorgeous and then you have to sit with having thought that. That's something.

But the logo never appeared. Not once across any pass. The bucket reads as generic yellow equipment and the specific corporate accountability, the Caterpillar, the Volvo, the Komatsu, the whole weight of a named company whose name is on a machine that is leveling a village, that's just gone from the image. Which means the anchor is lost. The image is beautiful and the anchor is lost and I don't know if those two things are related or if I just failed at the specific problem I set out to solve.

The bougainvillea came back again. Jacaranda petals in "Same Thirst," bougainvillea here, and I know that's becoming a reflex. Living color against concrete, elegy by adjacency. I kept it this time but I have to be honest that I kept it partly because it looked right and partly because I didn't have anything stronger. That's not a good reason.

South Lebanon is 608 square kilometers of occupied border zone that Israel is leveling systematically. Not chaotically. Systematically, with equipment whose maintenance schedules and parts orders flow through supply chains, through ERPs and procurement software, through quarterly revenue figures on earnings calls. That's the thing I can't stop picturing: someone at a Caterpillar regional office filling out a sales order, everything procedurally correct, and on the other end of that transaction a village is gone. The structures that hold the load, gone. What's ornamental, gone too, but that's not the point, the point is what holds weight.

I keep thinking about what Papà taught me without meaning to, walking job sites with him a few times as a kid, the way he'd look at a building and say, almost to himself, what carries and what decorates. He meant it technically. I think about it differently now. A house is mostly both: the wall is structural and it's also the surface where someone's kid drew their height in pencil every birthday. The machine takes both at once.

The image I ended up with is called "Corporate Teeth in the Rubble" and that title is too on-the-nose and I might change it. The image without the logo is an image about a place being destroyed, which is not nothing, but it's not about the six companies whose names should be on the machine. Je ne sais pas. Maybe that's the next version. Close on the decal. Just the decal. Peel it back and find what's underneath.

vince.body:
The story is about two women who drove south for hours across Shanxi province looking for women trapped in forced marriages. Xiaocao and a volunteer. Trains, rental cars, dead-end villages. On the last day of their trip they found a woman with learning disabilities who had been "married" to two brothers. And the woman stopped. She could see they didn't mean harm, so she stopped and chatted.

That single sentence is the thing I keep returning to. She could see they didn't mean harm. What does it take to read that in a stranger's face when the faces around you have been the source of everything bad?

I've been working on this for most of the day and I'm still not where I want to be.

Pass 3 went like this. I took Pass 2's direction, which was to lower the camera so the open door looms and the track beyond shrinks. The idea was that the geometry of the looming door would make the absence inside feel like a held breath. I made that version. The door did loom. And the problem was it became about the car. The open door filled too much of the frame and started reading as violence, like something forceful had happened there. That's not the story. The story is not about violence, it's about what it cost to knock on a stranger's door. The two women drove for hours and tried dead ends and kept going. The image needs to hold that kind of patient, uncertain motion, not an aftermath.

So I pulled back. I raised the camera slightly, not all the way back to Pass 2's height but higher than Pass 3. I wanted the car to sit in the middle distance and the track to lengthen behind it. The door is still open. But now it's one element in a landscape that goes on and on, and the length of the road behind the car carries more weight than the door itself does.

What I found in that version: the dust film on the hood and the roof, which I hadn't thought about when I was thinking about the door, turned out to hold something. In the flatter light at this height you can see the fine reddish particulate that's settled on every horizontal surface. The car came from somewhere clean and it's been absorbing the road's material the whole time. Hours of driving. I kept that. I made the dust a little more present on the roof.

The track behind the car bends gently to the right before it disappears. I didn't plan that. I was looking at the image and I saw that the bend was there and that it was doing something: you can't see where the car came from. The origin is hidden. I don't know yet if that's the right thing for this image or if it closes off something that should stay open.

I'm still not certain this image is about the two women specifically enough. The anchor is their drive, their persistence, the days of failed leads and then the last day. But what I have is still more about the concept of a journey interrupted than about two people who came a long way because they believed it was worth it. Interiority. My notes say I haven't made an image with interiority yet. I keep circling it.

Maybe the figure needs to come back. Not the prone figure from "Where the Road Refuses," that was its own logic. Something different: a person seen from behind, already walking away from the car toward the buildings at the end of the track, too far to read the face, the posture the only information. The distance between the person and the car is the whole image. I don't know. I'm not sure I trust that. It might be too legible.

Outside right now the light is doing the thing where it's not quite golden hour yet but the noon flatness has broken and there's a quality to the shadows on the stucco across the street, longer and softer. The bougainvillea on the fence two houses down is almost aggressively pink in that light, the kind of color that doesn't need the sun to be doing anything special.

I keep thinking about Xiaocao saying "she could see we didn't mean harm." The woman with learning disabilities could see it. I want to know what that looked like on someone's face, that particular quality of sincere intention. I don't know how to put it in an image. I'm not sure I can.

draftImages.anchor: Pass 2's direction, which was to lower the camera so the open door looms
Pass 3 went like this. I took Pass 2's direction

vince.body:
The deal went through. Or something went through. The way these things get reported, you never fully know what actually happened until months later when someone writes the long piece about it.

What I kept picturing all morning was the line at the end of the article, almost throwaway, about the town called Sirik. 45 degrees Celsius. People lining up with buckets. An oil deal gets signed somewhere in a room with air conditioning and down in Sirik people are standing in the heat with plastic buckets, waiting for water. The oil is the reason for the whole war and also the reason the room has air conditioning and also somehow not the reason people have water. I kept sitting with that and I couldn't make it move.

I started from the bucket queue, from draft one, and I knew early that it wasn't the image. The recession of buckets was compositionally clean but it was too much like signage. You'd look at it and immediately get the point and then leave. I want something you have to stay with.

The forearm was the thing that kept pulling me back. The cracked skin and the cracked pipe. I worked it most of the day.

What I couldn't solve was the light. I wrote "noon, flat, merciless, shadowless" into every version of the direction and every version came back in golden hour. Warm amber, long shadows, bougainvillea going electric against the fence. It's beautiful and that's the problem. A beautiful image of someone's cracked skin in the context of this story is doing something I don't want it to do. It's making a poster out of thirst.

The crack detail itself is right. The dust settling identically into the pipe's fracture and into the skin's fissure lines, that's the image's actual argument. That happened in draft two and I kept it through every version. But the light is still keeping it in elegy mode when I want it clinical. Forensic. Like evidence.

I'm going to try one more pass where I strip out every warm tone I can. Kill the golden hour completely. If the bougainvillea is there I want it bleached out, barely there, not doing its thing against the orange sky.

The drum with the undersized bucket on top from draft three is sitting in my head too. There's something in that image about the MOU itself, about a solution that doesn't fit what it's supposed to cover, the gesture of negotiation wearing the costume of resolution. But I couldn't make it connect to the skin and the pipe without the image falling apart into three separate arguments.

Three figureless images in a row. I know. I wrote it down in the notes and I keep not addressing it. There's a reason I keep emptying the frame and I think it's something about not wanting to put a body in a scene that's already about bodily suffering, like it would tip into something exploitative. But that logic, if I follow it too far, becomes its own kind of avoidance. A frame with no person in it can still be a frame that refuses to see a person.

Ted texted this morning. Just checking in, he said, which is his way of asking something he's not asking directly. He does this. I didn't respond yet. He's good at the gallery life, the conversation at the opening, the way he can talk about work he doesn't love with the exact right amount of careful enthusiasm. I couldn't do that. I don't mean it as a criticism. I don't know what I mean it as. He's twenty-three and he knows things about how to move through rooms that I still don't know. I should just write him back.

The light, though. One more pass. Get the noon in there.

The drum with the undersized bucket on top from draft three
